code: src/math_utils.py with 8 functions (weighted_average, clamp, is_in_range, safe_sqrt, normalize_to_unit, percentile_rank, batch_process)
corruptions: safe_sqrt: if x < 0 → if x > 0 (condition inverted)
clamp: if value < lo → if value > lo (condition inverted)
is_in_range: >= lo and <= hi → > lo and < hi (boundary exclusion)
batch_process: if items[i] > threshold → if items[i] >= threshold (off-by-one)
percentile_rank: if v < target → if v <= target (off-by-one)
key_findings: DeepSeek-Chat shows clearest difficulty scaling: 100%→75%→60% bug fix rate at 4-5 corruptions
Smaller models (2B) struggle even at 1 corruption — floor effect
Test pass rate is misleading — bugs that don't affect test output get 'accidentally' fixed
Bug fix rate is the better metric: measures whether the model actually found and reverted the corruption
Current benchmark too easy: 8 simple functions, 5 simple single-line bugs. Need real repos with cascading multi-file bugs.
next_steps: Test on real GitHub repos (pandas, scikit-learn, etc.) using RepoAnalyzer
Add cascading/masking dependencies (bug B hides bug A, fixing B reveals A)
Add multi-file corruptions (bugs across src/, tests/, config/)
Test with 7-10 sequential corruptions to find the true 'horizon limit' per model
Compare with SWE-bench style tasks for calibration
